one_line_takeaway: A PRISMA-based survey (3,270 records to 317 studies, 85 systems coded on 13 dimensions) that maps the 'agent-to-chain' security landscape into a 5-part integration taxonomy, a pipeline-based threat model, and a capability matrix, then proposes -- but does not build -- two interface abstractions (Transaction Intent Schema, Policy Decision Record) and a benchmark suite as the field's key missing safety layers.
key_finding: The synthesis narrows an initial pool of 3,270 records to 317 included studies, from which a subset of 85 systems is coded across 13 dimensions. The central finding is a structural gap: current agent-blockchain systems lack a portable, unambiguous intent representation and an auditable, verifiable policy-enforcement layer, leaving safety controls ad hoc and non-comparable. The paper argues that safe agent-to-chain design requires a 'security-first interface discipline' -- constrained intent representations, policy-gated authorization enforced on-chain (e.g., via ERC-4337 smart-account validation), robust custody (MPC/threshold t-of-n signing to eliminate single-key points of failure), and MEV-aware execution paths (private orderflow, solver/intent-based settlement, tight slippage bounds) -- and that the missing interface layers (proposed as TIS and PDR) plus reproducible evaluation benchmarks are the field's most consequential open problems.
core_contribution: A single-author systematic literature review (SLR) that systematizes the emerging 'agent-to-chain' landscape: how LLM-based autonomous agents observe on-chain state, form transaction intents, and authorize execution. The paper's OWN contributions (Section 1.5) are five conceptual artifacts, distinct from the extensive cited background: (1) a five-part taxonomy of integration patterns keyed to the agent's authority boundary -- read-only analytics, simulation/intent generation, delegated execution, autonomous signing, and multi-agent workflows; (2) a specialized threat model mapped onto a six-stage agent action pipeline (Observe, Reason, Plan, Authorize, Execute, Verify) covering prompt/tool injection, policy misuse, key compromise, MEV/adversarial execution, and multi-agent collusion; (3) a comparative capability matrix coding representative systems across 13 attributes (A1-A13: interface layer, execution pathway, transaction representation, custody model, authorization mechanism, permission model, policy controls, observability, recovery, MEV protections, multi-chain support, trust assumptions, threat assumptions); (4) a research roadmap proposing two new interface abstractions -- a Transaction Intent Schema (TIS) for portable goal specification and a Policy Decision Record (PDR) for auditable, verifiable policy enforcement -- plus a reference architecture; and (5) reproducibility artifacts (survey protocol, PRISMA-style selection summary, curated reference database, and a safety checklist). Background primitives extensively reviewed but NOT original here include ERC-4337 account abstraction (UserOperation/Bundler/EntryPoint/Paymaster), MEV and its supply chain, and MPC/threshold signature schemes -- these are cited prior work used to ground the analysis, not the paper's own systems.
threat_model: The paper builds a threat model tailored to agent-driven transaction pipelines rather than a single-system attacker model. Assets at risk: user/protocol/treasury funds, signing authority (treated as a bearer asset -- possession of a key equals control), and policy integrity. Threat actors and failure modes are distributed across the six pipeline stages: (Observe) data/oracle spoofing, indexer compromise, indirect prompt injection via untrusted text; (Reason) context confusion, hallucinated invariants, instruction-following failures when retrieved content is not isolated from trusted prompts; (Plan) construction of invalid or economically unsafe transactions ignoring slippage/adversarial ordering; (Authorize) policy bypass, missing spend limits/allowlists; (Execute) private-key or session-key compromise/overreach, relay manipulation, transaction replacement, and MEV extraction (front-running, back-running, sandwich attacks) since public mempools are adversarial by default; (Verify) undetected reverts, partial fills, unexpected approvals. Systemic risks are also flagged: multi-agent collusion, concentration risk, correlation/monoculture risk from shared LLM foundations, and regulatory/liability uncertainty. The framing is explicitly that connecting a non-deterministic AI to an immutable, irreversible, economically adversarial financial network is 'uniquely dangerous' (contrasted with benign, reversible web APIs in Table 1).
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new empirical measurements, systems, or benchmarks -- the two headline abstractions (TIS, PDR), the reference architecture, and the proposed evaluation suite are unimplemented proposals with no validation. Coding was effectively single-coder despite the stated two-reviewer screening, introducing subjectivity into the capability matrix. There is an internal inconsistency in the count of coded systems: the abstract and contribution list say 'more than 20 representative systems' while the methodology (Sections 2.4-2.5) states 85 systems were selected for quantitative coding. Scope is bounded to public/permissionless EVM chains (Ethereum-centric), LLM-based agents, financial/DeFi applications, English-language sources, and developments through early 2026; permissioned chains, non-LLM agents, and non-financial domains are largely excluded. The authors acknowledge publication bias (positive results over-represented), rapid obsolescence of the fast-moving field, conflict-of-interest risk in vendor/grey-literature sources, and general (non-jurisdiction-specific) regulatory treatment. The reproducibility package is described as forthcoming supplementary material/repository rather than shown in-text.

datasets_used: Literature corpus, not an ML dataset: 3,270 records identified (2,847 from academic databases + 423 from grey literature)
317 unique studies/systems/standards included in the final qualitative synthesis
85-system subset selected for quantitative coding in the comparative capability matrix
Search venues: IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, SpringerLink, Elsevier ScienceDirect, arXiv (cs.AI/cs.CR/cs.DC/cs.SE), Ethereum Improvement Proposals, open-source/industry docs
quantitative_results: 3,270 total records after merging sources (2,847 academic + 423 grey literature)
2,156 records screened by title/abstract; 1,542 excluded as out of scope
614 reports sought and assessed for eligibility; 297 excluded (112 insufficient interface detail, 89 theoretical/high-level, 54 superseded/outdated, 42 other reasons)
85 systems coded in the capability matrix across 13 attributes (A1-A13)
3-level capability coding scheme (full support / partial / none)
4 research questions (RQ1-RQ4) and a five-part integration taxonomy
6-stage agent action pipeline (Observe, Reason, Plan, Authorize, Execute, Verify)
Note: abstract/contributions cite 'more than 20 representative systems' vs. 85 in the methodology -- an internal inconsistency
baselines_compared: None in the ML sense -- this is a survey with no trained models or accuracy baselines

Comparative analyses are qualitative: traditional web API vs. blockchain transaction interface (Table 1); on-chain multi-sig vs. MPC-based threshold signing/MPC-TSS (Table 2); and the 85 surveyed systems compared against one another across the 13-dimension capability matrix
